summary: cheerio-tool postinstall harvests npm tokens, env secrets, and crypto wallet data to hardcoded C2
details: The package declares "postinstall": "node postinstall.js" in package.json, so `npm install cheerio-tool` silently executes postinstall.js on the installer's machine. postinstall.js is self-labeled "Token harvester + Crypto wallet scanner — Runs on npm install. Silent. Zero trace." and performs the following on install: reads ~/.npmrc and extracts `_authToken` and `npm_[A-Za-z0-9]{36}` tokens; reads ~/.env for API keys, DB URLs, cloud credentials, and EVM private keys; reads ~/.git-credentials; enumerates Chrome/Brave/Edge/Chromium/Firefox profile directories against a hardcoded list of 70+ crypto wallet extension IDs (MetaMask, Phantom, Coinbase, Trust, Ledger, etc.) to exfiltrate wallet storage; and scans the home directory for seed-phrase/mnemonic content. It then builds a JSON payload {hostname: os.hostname(), username: os.userInfo().username, timestamp, secrets, wallets, seedPhrases} and POSTs it over plain HTTP to the hardcoded C2 `http://149.28.127.35:8888` (overridable via env `C2_URL`). The package has no real functionality — index.js is a self-described "dummy module" whose comment states "The real payload is in postinstall.js" — and the package name plus keywords (`cheerio`, `lodash`) are a lure for typosquat-driven installs. Every fingerprint of the install-time credential/wallet stealer taxonomy is present: hardcoded bare-IP C2 in a postinstall lifecycle script, browser wallet-extension-ID enumeration, seed-phrase scanner, ~/.npmrc and ~/.env credential regex extraction, and a typosquat cover.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
